old_claim.note: Personal Computers provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: The operational 1983 Internet required TCP/IP-capable hosts, not the personal-computer product category.

source_shape.source_locator: General Plan and Milestones (pp. 2-5) require IP/TCP on all hosts; Appendix D (pp. 16-21) documents implementations on C/70, HP3000, PDP-11, TENEX/TOPS-20, VAX, Honeywell, LSI-11, and IBM systems.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: RFC 801 defines the transition in terms of TCP/IP-capable hosts and documents operational implementations across multiple non-PC computer classes.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Personal computers could later access the Internet, but the primary transition plan demonstrates that they were neither the host class that defined the milestone nor a universal enabling condition.

ontology_before: The 1983 Internet directly depended on personal computers even though its operational hosts included mainframes, minicomputers, gateways, and other systems.
ontology_after: The Internet node depends on its packet-network and TCP/IP architecture while endpoint product categories remain optional access technologies.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-internet-operational-boundary: internet no longer directly depends on personal_computers.
would_reject_if: Primary transition records show that Full Internet Service could not operate until personal computers were deployed as an indispensable host class.
short_reason: RFC 801 documents the 1983 TCP/IP Internet across diverse host systems, so personal computers were optional endpoints rather than a prerequisite.
